stem: Which of the following best describes what the keyword `this` refers to when used inside an instance method?

options.label: A
options.text: The class that declares the method

options.label: B
options.text: The current object on which the method was invoked

options.label: C
options.text: The most recently created object of the class

options.label: D
options.text: The parameter list passed to the method

explanation: Inside any instance method or constructor, `this` is a reference to the specific object on which the call was made. A describes the class itself, not an object. C is wrong because `this` tracks the invoked instance, not the creation order. D is wrong; parameters are local variables, not the object.

stem: Which of the following situations requires the use of `this` to correctly assign an instance variable inside a constructor?

options.label: A
options.text: When the constructor has no parameters

options.label: B
options.text: When a constructor parameter has the same name as an instance variable

options.label: C
options.text: When the instance variable is declared `public`

options.label: D
options.text: When the constructor is called from a static method

explanation: When a parameter shares a name with an instance variable, the parameter shadows the field inside the constructor body. `this.fieldName` explicitly refers to the instance variable. A requires no disambiguation because there is no shadowing. C and D are irrelevant; visibility and the call site have no bearing on the need for `this`.

options.label: A
options.text: It correctly assigns the parameter value to the instance variable `score`

options.label: B
options.text: It causes a compile-time error because `score` is ambiguous

options.label: C
options.text: It assigns the parameter `score` to itself, leaving the instance variable unchanged

options.label: D
options.text: It resets the instance variable `score` to its default value of `0`

explanation: Both occurrences of `score` inside the body resolve to the local parameter, which shadows the instance variable. The statement `score = score` is a self-assignment and is a no-op; the instance variable is never touched and keeps its prior value. No compile-time error occurs. D is wrong: the field is simply left alone, not explicitly zeroed.

options.label: D
options.text: A compile-time error occurs

explanation: The constructor body `brightness = brightness;` assigns the parameter to itself due to shadowing; the instance variable `brightness` is never set and retains the default `int` value of `0`. A would be correct only if `this.brightness = brightness;` were used. C and D do not occur.

stem: Which of the following statements about the `this` keyword in Java is TRUE?

options.label: A
options.text: `this` can be used inside a `static` method to refer to the class itself

options.label: B
options.text: `this` can be passed as an argument when calling another method

options.label: C
options.text: `this` can only appear in constructors, not in regular instance methods

options.label: D
options.text: `this` refers to the most recently instantiated object of the enclosing class

explanation: `this` is a valid object reference that may be passed as an argument — for example, `otherObject.someMethod(this)`. A is false: static methods have no associated object, so `this` is not defined in a static context and produces a compile-time error. C is false: `this` is valid in any instance method. D is false: `this` always tracks the specific invoked instance, not creation order.

options.label: A
options.text: `seconds = seconds;`

options.label: B
options.text: `this.seconds = seconds;`

options.label: C
options.text: `Timer.seconds = seconds;`

options.label: D
options.text: `int seconds = seconds;`

explanation: `this.seconds = seconds;` uses `this` to refer to the instance variable, assigning it the parameter's value. A has the shadowing bug — the parameter is assigned to itself. C causes a compile-time error because `seconds` is an instance field, not a static field, and cannot be accessed via the class name. D declares a new local variable and has no effect on the instance field.

The constructor sets `this.count = 10`. In `add`, `this.count` refers to the field while `count` refers to the parameter. After `c.add(5)`, the field is `15`; after `c.add(3)`, it is `18`. A ignores both calls; B reflects only the first; D would result from adding 5 and 3 without the initial 10.

`w1.transfer(w2)` runs with `this = w1` (dollars = 50) and `other = w2` (dollars = 20). First, `other.dollars += this.dollars` → w2.dollars = 70. Then `this.dollars = 0` → w1.dollars = 0. A method may legally access private fields of another instance of the same class. A ignores the call entirely; B applies only the zeroing; D applies only the addition.

options.label: A
options.text: `true`

options.label: B
options.text: `false`

options.label: C
options.text: A compile-time error occurs because `other.radius` accesses a private field

options.label: D
options.text: A runtime error occurs

explanation: `c2.isBiggerThan(c1)` sets `this = c2` (radius 3.0) and `other = c1` (radius 5.0). `this.radius > other.radius` evaluates to `3.0 > 5.0`, which is `false`. C is a common misconception: a method may legally access the private fields of a parameter whose declared type is the same class. No runtime error occurs.

After `p1.combine(p2)`: p1.score = 30 + 40 = 70, p2.score = 0. After `p2.combine(p1)`: p2.score = 0 + 70 = 70, p1.score = 0. Output is `0 70`. A reflects only the first call's state. B would require neither object to be zeroed during the second call. D ignores both calls.

`a.merge(b)` calls `this.deposit(other.balance)`, i.e., `a.deposit(200)`. Inside `deposit`, the parameter is named `balance`, shadowing the field; `this.balance += balance` correctly adds 200 to the field, giving `a.balance = 300`. Then `other.balance = 0` sets `b.balance = 0`. `toString()` concatenates the field, so output is `300 0`. A ignores all changes; B skips zeroing `b`; D skips the deposit step.

stem: Which of the following best describes the purpose of the `this` keyword when used inside an instance method?

options.label: A
options.text: It refers to the class in which the method is declared.

options.label: B
options.text: It refers to the current object on which the method was called.

options.label: C
options.text: It creates a new instance of the current class.

options.label: D
options.text: It allows the method to call static methods defined in the same class.

explanation: `this` is a reference to the current object — the specific instance on which the method is being invoked. It is not the class itself (A), does not construct a new object (C), and has no special relationship to static methods (D).

The constructor runs `this.size = 4 * 2 = 8`, setting the field to 8. Inside `expand(3)`, the statement `size = this.size + size` computes `8 + 3 = 11` and assigns it to the local parameter `size` — not to `this.size`. The field is unchanged. `getSize()` returns the instance field, so the output is `8`. Option C results from incorrectly believing `expand` modifies the field.

`s.reading` starts at `20`. `selfCalibrate()` calls `calibrate(this)`, passing `s` as `other`. Inside `calibrate`, Java evaluates the right-hand side first: `other.reading + 10 = 20 + 10 = 30`, then assigns `30` to `this.reading`. Since `this` and `other` are the same object, `s.reading` is now `30`. `getReading()` returns `30`. Option B results from thinking that passing an object to itself has no observable effect; option D would require the operation to execute twice.

stem: Inside an instance method of a class, what does the keyword `this` refer to?

options.label: A
options.text: The class definition (blueprint) from which the current object was created

options.label: B
options.text: The current object — the specific instance on which the method is being executed

options.label: C
options.text: The first object argument passed to the current method

options.label: D
options.text: The return value of the most recently called method in the program

explanation: `this` is always a reference to the current object — the instance on which the method was invoked. A describes the class itself, not an object. C confuses `this` with a parameter. D is entirely fabricated; `this` has nothing to do with return values.

stem: Which of the following statements about the `this` keyword in Java is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: `this` can be used inside both instance methods and static methods of a class.

options.label: B
options.text: `this` can be used to distinguish between a parameter and an instance variable that share the same name.

options.label: C
options.text: `this` refers to the class itself rather than to any particular object.

options.label: D
options.text: `this` must be written explicitly every time an instance variable is accessed inside a method.

explanation: When a parameter shadows an instance variable of the same name, writing `this.name` targets the field while `name` alone refers to the parameter — this disambiguation is the primary use of `this`. A is wrong because static methods have no implicit object and therefore no `this`. C confuses `this` with the class name. D is wrong because `this` is only necessary when shadowing creates an ambiguity.

The constructor sets `this.x = 3` and `this.y = 4`. Calling `p.shift(2, -1)` executes `this.x += 2` (x → 5) and `this.y += -1` (y → 4 + (−1) = 3). `println(p)` calls `toString()`, printing `"5,3"`. A results if `shift` is ignored. B gives only the parameter values. D results if `this.y += y` were mistakenly read as `this.y = y` (assignment instead of compound addition).

options.label: A
options.text: `getBalance()` returns `100`; `getBonus()` returns `10`

options.label: B
options.text: `getBalance()` returns `150`; `getBonus()` returns `10`

options.label: C
options.text: `getBalance()` returns `150`; `getBonus()` returns `15`

options.label: D
options.text: `getBalance()` returns `50`; `getBonus()` returns `10`

explanation: The constructor sets `this.balance = 100` and `this.bonus = 100 / 10 = 10`. In `deposit(50)`, the parameter is also named `balance`; `this.balance += balance` correctly adds 50 to the field, making it 150. The `bonus` field is only assigned in the constructor and does not automatically update, so it stays 10. C results from incorrectly believing `bonus` recomputes to `150/10=15`. D results from misreading `+=` as `=`.

In the constructor, `size = size;` assigns the parameter to itself, so the instance variable remains its default value `0`. The mutator uses `this.size`, so it correctly changes the instance variable to `7`.

options.label: A
options.text: `number = number; owner = owner;`

options.label: B
options.text: `this.number = number; this.owner = owner;`

options.label: C
options.text: `number = this.number; owner = this.owner;`

options.label: D
options.text: `this.number = this.number; this.owner = this.owner;`

explanation: `this.number` and `this.owner` refer to the instance variables, while `number` and `owner` refer to the constructor parameters. The other choices either assign parameters to themselves or leave the fields unchanged.
